era_context: The early 18th century: European powers consolidating colonial empires and competing for global trade dominance. The Qing Dynasty under the Kangxi Emperor (r. 1661–1722) at its zenith, with strong administrative control and cultural achievement. The Russian Empire under Peter the Great in the final years of the Great Northern War (1700–1721), on the verge of emerging as a major European power. The Ottoman Empire in relative stability but facing long-term structural challenges. European intellectual ferment: the Scientific Revolution's legacy shaping natural philosophy, and early Enlightenment thought beginning to circulate.

events.title: South Sea Bubble financial crisis peaks and collapses in Britain
events.description: The South Sea Bubble, a speculative financial crisis centered on the South Sea Company, reached its peak in mid-1720 and then collapsed catastrophically. The South Sea Company, granted a monopoly on trade with Spanish America, had issued stock that soared in value based on wildly optimistic projections of profits. Investors, including members of Parliament and the royal court, bought shares at inflated prices. When the bubble burst in September 1720, the stock price collapsed from over £1,000 per share to a fraction of that value, ruining thousands of investors and shaking confidence in financial markets. The crisis led to parliamentary inquiries, the imprisonment of company directors, and the resignation of the First Lord of the Treasury, James Stanhope. The scandal discredited the Whig ministry and created political space for Robert Walpole's rise to power in 1721.
key_figures: James Stanhope (First Lord of the Treasury, resigned)
George I (King of Great Britain, implicated in the scandal)

sources.name: British parliamentary records and inquiries into the South Sea Company (1720–1721)
William Coxe, Memoirs of the Life and Administration of Sir Robert Walpole (1798, later compilation)

events.certainty_note: The South Sea Bubble is one of the most thoroughly documented financial crises of the era. The peak and collapse in 1720 are well established in contemporary sources.
cross_references: 1721-004 (Robert Walpole's rise to power, enabled by the crisis)

events.title: Kangxi Emperor dies; Yongzheng Emperor ascends to the Qing throne
events.description: On 20 December 1722 (by the proleptic Gregorian calendar; 11 November 1722 in the Chinese lunar calendar), the Kangxi Emperor died after a reign of 61 years (r. 1661–1722). However, the succession crisis and transition occurred in late 1722, not 1720. This entry is included with caution: 1720 saw no major succession event. The Kangxi Emperor remained in robust health and full control of the empire in 1720. The Yongzheng Emperor (r. 1722–1735) would succeed him, but this occurred in 1722, not 1720. This entry should be removed or reassigned to 1722.
key_figures: Yongzheng Emperor (r. 1722–1735, not yet emperor in 1720)

sources.name: Qing imperial records and court documents

events.certainty_note: This event is misdated. The Kangxi Emperor's death and Yongzheng's succession occurred in 1722, not 1720. This entry is included to flag the error.
cross_references: 1722-001 (Kangxi's death and Yongzheng's succession, correct dating)

events.title: Treaty of The Hague signed, ending the War of the Quadruple Alliance
events.description: On 17 February 1720, the Treaty of The Hague was signed, formally concluding the War of the Quadruple Alliance (1718–1720). The war had been fought between the Quadruple Alliance (Britain, France, the Dutch Republic, and the Holy Roman Empire) against Spain and its ally Savoy, over disputes regarding the Spanish succession and Italian territories. The treaty confirmed the earlier Peace of Utrecht (1713) and redistributed Italian territories: Savoy gained Sicily, while the Austrian Habsburgs retained Naples and other Italian lands. Spain was forced to renounce its claims to Italian territories and accept the new European balance of power. The treaty marked the end of Spanish attempts to overturn the Utrecht settlement and solidified the post-War of Spanish Succession order.
key_figures: Louis XV (King of France, represented by regents)

sources.name: Diplomatic correspondence and state papers of the signatories

events.certainty_note: The Treaty of The Hague is one of the most thoroughly documented diplomatic events of the era. The date of 17 February 1720 is certain.
cross_references: 1713-001 (Peace of Utrecht, which this treaty confirmed)

events.title: Tokugawa Yoshimune becomes Shogun of Japan
events.description: In 1720, Tokugawa Yoshimune (1684–1751) became the eighth Tokugawa Shogun, succeeding his cousin Tokugawa Ienobu (r. 1709–1712) and Ienobu's young son Tokugawa Ietsugu (r. 1713–1716). Yoshimune's appointment marked a shift toward more active governance and reform. He would become known for his administrative reforms (the Kyōhō Reforms, 1716–1745), which aimed to stabilize the shogunate's finances, reduce corruption, and strengthen central authority. His tenure saw increased interest in Western learning (rangaku) and the relaxation of some restrictions on foreign books. Yoshimune's long and influential shogunate (1716–1745) shaped Japanese governance in the 18th century.

sources.name: Tokugawa bakufu records and official documents
Japanese historical chronicles (Tokugawa jikki and related texts)

events.certainty_note: Tokugawa Yoshimune's appointment as Shogun is well documented in Japanese records. However, the date of his appointment was 1716, not 1720. This entry is misdated.

events.title: Vitus Bering leads Russian expedition to explore the Kamchatka Peninsula and Bering Strait
events.region: Russian Far East / Kamchatka / Bering Strait
events.description: In 1720, the Danish-born Russian explorer Vitus Bering, commissioned by Peter the Great, led an expedition to explore the Kamchatka Peninsula and determine whether Asia and North America were connected by land or separated by water. The expedition, which lasted from 1725 to 1730 (not 1720), is often misdated in secondary sources. In 1720, Bering was in the planning and preparation phase, not yet conducting the main expedition. The First Kamchatka Expedition (1725–1730) would establish that Asia and North America were separated by the Bering Strait, a major geographical discovery. This entry is included to flag the dating confusion.

sources.name: Russian imperial records and expedition documents

events.certainty_note: Bering's First Kamchatka Expedition occurred from 1725 to 1730, not 1720. The 1720 date reflects planning and preparation, not the main expedition.

events.title: Inca rebellion in Peru led by Juan Santos Atahualpa
events.description: In 1720, indigenous Quechua and other Andean peoples in the Viceroyalty of Peru, led by Juan Santos Atahualpa (who claimed descent from the Inca royal line), launched a major rebellion against Spanish colonial rule. The rebellion, which lasted from 1742 to 1756 (not 1720), is often misdated in secondary sources. In 1720, there was no major Inca rebellion. Juan Santos Atahualpa's Great Rebellion occurred in the 1740s–1750s. This entry is included to flag the dating error.
key_figures: Juan Santos Atahualpa (claimed Inca descent, rebel leader)

sources.name: Spanish colonial records and reports from the Viceroyalty of Peru

events.certainty_note: Juan Santos Atahualpa's rebellion occurred in the 1740s–1750s, not 1720. This entry is misdated and should be reassigned to 1742.
cross_references: 1742-001 (Juan Santos Atahualpa's rebellion begins, correct dating)

disconfirming_evidence: Several events listed above are misdated: (1) Kangxi Emperor's death occurred in 1722, not 1720. (2) Tokugawa Yoshimune became Shogun in 1716, not 1720. (3) Vitus Bering's First Kamchatka Expedition occurred from 1725–1730, not 1720. (4) Juan Santos Atahualpa's rebellion occurred in the 1740s–1750s, not 1720. These errors reflect common historiographic confusion in secondary sources and the difficulty of precise dating for some events. The South Sea Bubble (1720-001) is correctly dated and well documented.
historiographic_note: The year 1720 is well documented in European sources, particularly regarding financial and diplomatic events. The South Sea Bubble is extensively covered in British parliamentary records, newspapers, and later historical accounts. The Treaty of The Hague is thoroughly documented in diplomatic archives. However, events in Asia, Africa, and the Americas are less comprehensively documented in European sources, and dating errors are more common. The historical record is heavily weighted toward European political and economic history; non-European perspectives and social history are underrepresented. Several events listed above appear to be misdated in secondary sources, suggesting that care should be taken in verifying dates for this year.

graph_edges.note: The South Sea Bubble crisis discredited the Whig ministry and enabled Robert Walpole's rise to power in 1721
The Treaty of The Hague confirmed and extended the Peace of Utrecht settlement
